ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Lababdar. FOODON_03302571 (cottage cheese food product) is used as the closest parent representing the primary ingredient (paneer/cottage cheese). A more specific curry preparation term is not available in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents paneer as a dairy product. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Paneer Lababdar as a prepared curry dish. This is the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Paneer Lababdar or a sufficiently close parent concept in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Lababdar is approximately 300 g, comprising paneer cubes in a rich tomato-onion-cream gravy, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer curry (Indian cottage cheese curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted with USDA FoodData Central reference values for paneer and tomato-cream gravy components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Paneer Lababdar is a North Indian restaurant preparation. ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for paneer (per 100 g: energy 265 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 20.8 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g) and for tomato-based curry gravies. The final per-100g composite is estimated for the prepared dish (paneer ~40%, tomato-cream gravy ~60%) using ICMR-NIN paneer values and USDA FoodData Central reference values for tomato-onion-cream gravy components. USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 01015 (cream) and NDB No. 11529 (tomatoes) used for gravy fraction. Composite weighted estimate applied. Source match type: adapted/estimated.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of paneer-based and dairy-rich Indian curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for paneer and mixed vegetable curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Lababdar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) paneer (Indian cottage cheese) has a very low GI (~15–27) due to high protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate; (2) the tomato-cream gravy contributes modest carbohydrate from tomatoes and onions (GI ~15–35); (3) the composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 35 (low category), consistent with analogous dairy-rich Indian curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 6.2 × 3) / 100 = 6.5 × 2 ≈ 13.0 g. This is a conservative estimate; actual GL may be lower.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from cream and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of Paneer Lababdar to 150–200 g per meal, as the sodium content (~960 mg per 300 g serving) approaches 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; request reduced salt and cream preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high protein and fat content of paneer with modest carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Lababdar has a low glycemic index (~35) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~13 g), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (150–200 g) and paired with low-GI accompaniments such as roti rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~7.2 g/100 g) from cream, butter, and paneer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content of Paneer Lababdar (~21.6 g per 300 g serving) is a concern for cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with CVD should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g), request preparation with reduced cream and butter, and balance with high-fibre accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Lababdar is a rich, indulgent North Indian vegetarian curry dish made from paneer (Indian cottage cheese) cooked in a thick, creamy tomato-onion gravy. The name 'Lababdar' derives from the Urdu/Hindi word conveying richness and indulgence. The dish is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic until golden, then blending with tomatoes to form a smooth base, which is enriched with cream, butter, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, red chilli, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). Cubes of fresh paneer are simmered in this gravy until they absorb the flavours. The result is a velvety, mildly spiced, tangy-sweet curry with a rich mouthfeel from the dairy components. Paneer Lababdar is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus and is popular across India, Pakistan, and the Indian diaspora worldwide, including in Singapore where it is served at Indian and fusion restaurants. Nutritionally, it is a good source of protein (8.5 g/100 g) and calcium (185 mg/100 g) from paneer, but is calorie-dense (168 kcal/100 g) due to cream and butter. The saturated fat content (~7.2 g/100 g) warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. Its low glycemic index (~35) makes it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. Commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Paneer Lababdar?
Is Paneer Lababdar good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Paneer Lababdar and Paneer Butter Masala?
What spices are in Paneer Lababdar?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Paneer Lababdar exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN paneer values and USDA FoodData Central reference values for cream and tomato-based gravy components. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database dairy-curry analogues. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (paneer) has well-established nutritional values in ICMR-NIN, and the gravy components are well-characterised in USDA FoodData Central.
notes: 1. Cuisine correction: The input JSON listed cuisine as 'Turkish', which is incorrect. Paneer Lababdar is a North Indian dish; the restaurant (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine) serves it as a menu item but the dish itself is Indian in origin. Cuisine corrected to 'Indian'. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates: paneer fraction (~40% by weight) from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), gravy fraction (~60%) from USDA FoodData Central cream and tomato references. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish category and is not reliably estimated from components. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for paneer or tomato-cream curry preparations. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database dairy-rich Indian curry analogues; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Lababdar exists in the approved GI database. 6. Sodium estimate (~320 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home preparations may be lower. 7. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is retained from the input as paneer is a protein-rich dairy alternative consistent with this classification. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data.
